[PDF_PAGE 12 | score=5] Monty Haul The relationship between risk and reward can influence the style of play more powerfully than alยญ most any other factor. In the "Monty Haul" giveaway camยญ paign, the players gain treasures and experience points in a disproยญ portionate ratio to the dangers overcome. With little personal risk, the characters greedily accumulate magic items, gems, and gold. They quickly attain uncontrollably high experience levels, rapidly advancing beyond the OMs ability to handle them. To accommodate such characters, the OM may find himยญ self hopelessly sacrificing armies of ores and dragons by the dozens, only to realize that this just gives these ridiculous characters even more ill-earned experie
[PDF_PAGE 9 | score=2] Dungeon Mastering is an art which takes a few minutes to Jearn and years to perfect. It goes beยญ yond world or adventure creation, beyond social grace, even beyond advanced crowd control. It comยญ bines vast amounts of creativity with the ability to make quic~ acยญ curate judgment calls. It requires a mind that can visualize situations as they happen and detennine the probable (and often highly nnprobยญ able) results of the actions taken by the players and their characยญ ters. The ultimate goal is to provide the players with gripping advenยญ ture within the fantastic realms of a shared imagination. Although all players (including the DM) contribute to the quality of the game, ultimate success dep
[PDF_PAGE 39 | score=2] "Oh boy, we grab all the magical loot and stick it in our portable holes. The wizards begin setting up the transmat box that teleports all the gold back to Castle Pardo." "Well, as you do that} the south wall glows and a humongous batยญ winged creature steps through, oozing pink goo from huge warts and dragging a huge bag of treaยญ sure." The television game show has become an integral part of our culยญ ture. With little or no personal risk (other than that of public humiliaยญ tion on nation-wide television), regular folks can try for hoards of valuable prizes. The winners walk out with all those things our Inateยญ rialistic souls crave and even the losers get consolation prizes. Some garners pla
[PDF_PAGE 98 | score=2] Mundane Folk There may be a tendency for the OM to role-play his "everyday" folk as if they were all adventurers, or at least ex-adventurers. They seem oblivious to the fact that they live in a world of monsters and magic and take the exotic in stride: "Dang dragon's in the backyard again Maw, go call us an advenยญ turer ta get 'im outa the cabbages. Do it m 'self? Lissen woman, I'm sortin 'out .spell components for market, I cain't be bothered! Git yor ass back in tha outhouse and clean me scrubbages fI Mundane folk are the way they are because the fantastic is not a part of their everyday life (if it was, it wouldn't be fantastic). It's hard to make monsters frightening or have great magics
[PDF_PAGE 11 | score=1] crawlers, even the most combatยญ crazed players may desire a change of pace. Thinker Sherlock Holmes, the Great Deยญ tective himself, would be comfortยญ able with this style of play. It encourages the players to think their way out of situations (often to the exclusion of other play opยญ tions). It is more a test of the playยญ ers' puzzle and problem solving skills than of their character's abilยญ ities. Taken to the extreme, this style of play presents a constant stream of enigmatic riddles, conยญ voluted logic puzzles, murky mysยญ teries, and a countless collection of tricky, troublesome traps. Remember, when designing problems and riddles, make them possible to solve. Fun quickly evaporates in an
[PDF_PAGE 19 | score=1] Controlling the pace of play may well be one of the most important steps toward becoming a master DM. In the same way that he deยญ signs his world and scenarios for maximum enjoyment, he must learn to orchestrate the compoยญ nents of game pacing. He must know when to speed up or slow down the play, throw out the rules for more enjoyment, or call a break for his fatigued players. Just as imยญ portantly, he must know when NOT to interrupt play. The OM must learn to be aware of nonverยญ bal feedback from his players and be able to understand what they are telling him. Such feedback might come in the fonn of yawns, interplayer chatter, or shortened tempers. While the goal of a game may be to have th
[PDF_PAGE 21 | score=1] rarely enjoyable by all parties inยญ volved. As the ending time draws near, the OM needs to watch for an obvious stopping point (again, inยญ terludes and cliff-hangers are best). As a warning though, the OM who sets up a cliff-hanger endยญ ing for his game session will find hilTIself under immediate pressure to resolve the situation. Everyone wants to know what happens next. It is better to quit while the players are still interested and excited, than to continue gaming past reaยญ sonable endurance. Scripting an Adventure It is possible to prepare (or at least attempt to prepare) the pace of the adventure in advance. If the DM can imagine his adventure as a story, then he should continue the ana
[PDF_PAGE 27 | score=1] covering the nature of their unยญ known foe than they are with learning how much treasure it is hiding. Keep the players guessing, keep them on edge, even make them afraid-they'll love you for it. Funny-Ha-Ha Humor, in all its various fonns can break the tension, relieve the fear, and proยญ vide a balance for all the intensity associated with cOInbat and life-orยญ death puzzle-solving. A joke (even outside the context of the game), a fwmy incident, or a silly NPC porยญ trayal can slow the pace of the game down and give the players enough time to regain their perยญ spective on a situation. Humor can also provide a deadly trap. Picture an inept NPC, someยญ one who could be dangerous if he had his wi
